title: The Anglo-Norman invasion
summary: English political involvement begins.
what: Anglo-Norman forces arrived and Henry II claimed authority over Ireland.
why: The intervention began centuries of contested English and later British rule.
colle: How far back must we go to understand a modern conflict?

title: The Flight of the Earls
summary: Gaelic political power collapses in Ulster.
what: Leading Gaelic lords left Ireland after defeat, opening the way for large-scale plantation.
why: The settlement of Protestant communities in Ulster shaped later unionism and partition.
colle: How did plantation shape modern identities in Ireland?

title: The Battle of the Boyne
summary: Williamite victory confirms Protestant political dominance.
what: William III defeated James II in a conflict tied to wider European struggles.
why: The battle remains a powerful symbol in Northern Irish unionist culture.
colle: Why do historical victories remain politically sensitive?

title: The Act of Union
summary: Ireland is incorporated into the United Kingdom.
what: The Irish Parliament was abolished and representation transferred to Westminster.
why: The Union created the institutional framework against which modern Irish nationalism developed.
colle: Did the Union make reform easier or independence inevitable?

title: Catholic Emancipation
summary: Most legal restrictions on Catholic political participation are removed.
what: Daniel O’Connell’s mass movement pressured Westminster to allow Catholics to sit in Parliament.
why: The campaign demonstrated the power of peaceful popular mobilisation.
colle: Can constitutional campaigning achieve revolutionary change?

title: The Great Famine
summary: Crop failure becomes a demographic catastrophe.
what: Potato blight caused hunger, disease and mass emigration while British policy responses proved gravely inadequate.
why: The Famine transformed population, diaspora and attitudes to British rule.
colle: Was the Famine a natural disaster or a political failure?

title: The Easter Rising
summary: A failed rebellion becomes the symbolic spark of independence.
what: Republicans seized key buildings in Dublin and proclaimed an Irish Republic before being defeated.
why: The execution of its leaders shifted public opinion towards separatism.
colle: Why can a military defeat become a political victory?

title: The War of Independence
summary: Guerrilla conflict challenges British rule.
what: The IRA fought British forces while the revolutionary Dáil claimed democratic legitimacy.
why: The conflict forced negotiations but also hardened divisions over sovereignty and violence.
colle: Can political violence be legitimate in an anti-colonial struggle?

title: Partition and the Anglo-Irish Treaty
summary: The island is divided and the Irish Free State is agreed.
what: The Treaty created a self-governing dominion while six counties remained in Northern Ireland.
why: Partition produced two states and left competing national identities unresolved.
colle: Was partition inevitable?

title: The Civil War
summary: Former allies fight over the Treaty.
what: Pro- and anti-Treaty forces fought over the status and limits of the new state.
why: The conflict shaped party politics for generations, especially Fianna Fáil and Fine Gael.
colle: Why did the Civil War structure Irish politics for so long?

title: The Constitution
summary: A new constitutional order asserts sovereignty.
what: Bunreacht na hÉireann created the office of President and defined the institutions of the state.
why: Frequent referendums have allowed the constitution to become a vehicle for social change.
colle: Does frequent constitutional voting strengthen democracy?

title: Ireland becomes a republic
summary: The state formally leaves the Commonwealth.
what: The Republic of Ireland Act ended the remaining external role of the British monarch.
why: The change completed constitutional separation while Northern Ireland remained within the UK.
colle: How important are symbols in completing independence?

title: Ireland joins the EEC
summary: European membership accelerates economic and social change.
what: Ireland entered the European Economic Community alongside the United Kingdom and Denmark.
why: Europe provided markets, funding and a counterweight to economic dependence on Britain.
colle: How did Europe transform Ireland’s place in the world?

title: The Anglo-Irish Agreement
summary: Dublin gains a consultative role in Northern Irish affairs.
what: The British and Irish governments agreed to closer cooperation despite unionist opposition.
why: It established habits of intergovernmental partnership that helped prepare the peace process.
colle: Can outside governments help resolve identity conflicts?

title: The Celtic Tiger
summary: Rapid growth transforms a once-poor economy.
what: Foreign investment, EU membership, education and a young workforce drove exceptional expansion.
why: Prosperity encouraged immigration and social confidence but also created dependence on property and multinational capital.
colle: Is Ireland’s economic model too dependent on multinational companies?

title: The Good Friday Agreement
summary: A new framework brings peace to Northern Ireland.
what: The agreement created power-sharing, cross-border institutions and consent-based constitutional rules.
why: It largely ended the Troubles and made cooperation between London, Dublin and Belfast central to stability.
colle: Is a united Ireland now more realistic?

title: The financial crash and bailout
summary: The Celtic Tiger collapses.
what: A property and banking crisis forced the state to guarantee banks and accept an EU-IMF assistance programme.
why: Austerity reshaped politics, housing and public trust.
colle: Did Ireland recover from the crash at too high a social cost?

title: Marriage equality referendum
summary: Ireland becomes the first country to legalise same-sex marriage by popular vote.
what: A decisive referendum amended the constitution to permit marriage regardless of sex.
why: The result symbolised rapid secularisation and generational change.
colle: How did Ireland liberalise so quickly?

title: Abortion referendum
summary: Voters repeal the constitutional ban on abortion.
what: The electorate removed the Eighth Amendment, allowing Parliament to legislate for abortion access.
why: The vote marked a major shift in women’s rights and the declining authority of the Catholic Church.
colle: Should fundamental rights be decided by referendum?

title: Sinn Féin’s electoral breakthrough
summary: The old two-party system is disrupted.
what: Sinn Féin won the largest share of first-preference votes, although Fianna Fáil, Fine Gael and the Greens formed a coalition.
why: Housing, reunification and generational change became central to a more fragmented party system.
colle: Is Ireland moving beyond its Civil War party system?

title: Election and a new coalition
summary: Fianna Fáil and Fine Gael remain at the centre of government.
what: After the November 2024 election, Micheál Martin became Taoiseach in January 2025 with support from Fianna Fáil, Fine Gael and independent deputies.
why: The outcome showed both continuity and pressure from housing shortages, migration debates and Sinn Féin’s challenge.
colle: Can centrist coalition government solve Ireland’s housing crisis?
